title: Lesson 33 – Imperative, Prohibitive & Indirect Speech
description: Befehlsform (命令形: ～ろ/～なさい), Verbotsform (禁止形: ～な), indirekte Rede (～と言っていました), Schilder und Anweisungen.

vocabulary.meaning: to make noise, to be rowdy
to convey, to pass on (a message)

grammar.title: 命令形 (Imperative Form: Do ~!)
grammar.structure: Group 1: -い → -え / Group 2: -ます → -ろ (or -なさい) / Group 3: しろ・こい
grammar.explanation: The imperative form is used to give direct commands. For Group 1, the final vowel changes to -e (e.g., 書きます → 書け). For Group 2, ます is replaced by ろ (e.g., 食べます → 食べろ). Group 3: します → しろ, きます → こい. This form sounds strong and is mainly used by men in informal situations, in emergencies, or in sports coaching. The polite imperative ～なさい (e.g., 食べなさい) is softer and often used by parents or teachers.
grammar.example_sentences:
① 早く 走れ！
  (Hayaku hashire!)
  — Run fast!
② ここに 座れ。
  (Koko ni suwere.)
  — Sit here.
③ 静かに しなさい。
  (Shizuka ni shinasai.)
  — Be quiet. (polite command)

grammar.title: 禁止形 (Prohibitive Form: Don't ~!)
grammar.explanation: The prohibitive form is made by adding な to the dictionary form of a verb. It means 'Don't do ~!' and is a strong, direct prohibition. It is commonly seen on signs (e.g., 入るな — Don't enter!) and used in urgent situations. In everyday speech, ～ないでください is the polite equivalent.
grammar.example_sentences:
④ ここに 入るな！
  (Koko ni hairu na!)
  — Don't enter here!
⑤ さわるな。危ない。
  (Sawaru na. Abunai.)
  — Don't touch it. It's dangerous.
⑥ 騒ぐな！
  (Sawagu na!)
  — Don't make noise!

grammar.title: ～と 言っていました (Indirect Speech: Someone said ~)
grammar.structure: Plain form + と 言っていました / と 言いました
grammar.explanation: This pattern is used to report what someone said (indirect speech). ～と言いました reports a one-time statement, while ～と言っていました reports something someone has been saying or said in the speaker's memory. The quoted part uses plain form. For conveying messages: ～と伝えてください (please tell them that ~).
grammar.example_sentences:
⑦ 田中さんは 明日 来ないと 言っていました。
  (Tanaka-san wa ashita konai to itte imashita.)
  — Mr. Tanaka said he won't come tomorrow.
⑧ 先生は「もっと 勉強しなさい」と 言いました。
  (Sensei wa 'motto benkyou shinasai' to iimashita.)
  — The teacher said, 'Study more.'
⑨ すみません、山田さんに 3時に 来てくださいと 伝えてください。
  (Sumimasen, Yamada-san ni san-ji ni kite kudasai to tsutaete kudasai.)
  — Excuse me, please tell Mr. Yamada to come at 3 o'clock.

grammar.title: 看板・標識の読み方 (Reading Signs & Instructions)
grammar.explanation: In Japan, signs and notices often use imperative and prohibitive forms, as well as special expressions. Common patterns include: 禁止 (prohibited), 注意 (caution), ～こと (instruction, e.g., 静かにすること — Be quiet), and ～べからず (classical prohibitive). Understanding these forms is essential for daily life in Japan.
grammar.example_sentences:
⑩ 「禁煙」— No smoking.
  (Kin'en)
⑪ 「立入禁止」— No entry.
  (Tachiiri kinshi)
⑫ 「非常口」— Emergency exit.
  (Hijouguchi)
⑬ 「ゴミを 捨てるな」— Don't throw away trash.
  (Gomi o suteru na)

conversation.title: Please pass on the message
conversation.title_ja: 伝言を お願いします

lines.speaker: Yamada
lines.japanese: すみません、中村さんは いらっしゃいますか。
lines.english: Excuse me, is Mr. Nakamura here?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.japanese: 中村は 今 外出しています。
lines.romaji: Nakamura wa ima gaishutsu shite imasu.

lines.speaker: Yamada
lines.japanese: そうですか。では、伝言を お願いできますか。
lines.romaji: Sou desu ka. Dewa, dengon o onegai dekimasu ka.
lines.english: I see. Could I leave a message then?

lines.speaker: Yamada
lines.japanese: 明日の 会議は 3時からに 変わったと 伝えてください。
lines.romaji: Ashita no kaigi wa san-ji kara ni kawatta to tsutaete kudasai.
lines.english: Please tell him the meeting tomorrow has changed to 3 o'clock.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.japanese: 会議が 3時からですね。わかりました。
lines.romaji: Kaigi ga san-ji kara desu ne. Wakarimashita.
lines.english: The meeting is from 3 o'clock, right. Understood.

lines.speaker: Yamada
lines.japanese: それから、資料を 忘れないでと 言っていたと 伝えてください。
lines.romaji: Sorekara, shiryou o wasurenaide to itte ita to tsutaete kudasai.
lines.english: Also, please tell him I said not to forget the documents.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.japanese: はい、必ず 伝えます。
lines.english: Yes, I will make sure to pass it on.

lines.speaker: Yamada
lines.japanese: よろしく お願いします。失礼します。
